commentary: Big news I have been sitting on for months: Off-Mic, my new podcast about the people and stories behind South African radio, launches 30 September 2026. Twelve years of conversations that never made it to air, finally getting their moment. More soon.

This morning on The Asha Show we opened the lines on what makes a city feel like home. Melville callers, you carried the whole hour. This is exactly why Voices of the City matters: radio is still the place where Johannesburg talks to itself.

Proud day. Our latest JMU Youth Media Mentorship cohort just wrapped their first live studio shift at Rhythm FM 94.7. One of them froze for a full three seconds, then read the traffic like she had done it for years. That three seconds is the whole job.
Honoured to be confirmed as a speaker at the National Association of Broadcasters gathering this year. My topic: why live radio still beats the algorithm. Come argue with me afterwards.
Spent the morning in the archive at Rhythm FM 94.7 pulling tape for the station's 30th Anniversary documentary. Thirty years of Gauteng on reel. Some of these jingles deserve to be in a museum.

Writing day. My book on South African radio culture is getting closer, and chapter four is all about the late-night graveyard shift hosts who taught the rest of us how to talk to one listener at a time.
Shout out to my producer Sipho Ndaba and sound engineer Naledi Tau. A morning drive show lives or dies on the team you cannot see. The Asha Show would be silence without them.

On air this week we are talking about how young presenters can build a career in South African radio without losing themselves. If you are starting out in broadcasting, what do you wish someone had told you on day one?

Recorded a beautiful Voices of the City segment in Soweto today with a taxi rank musician who has been busking the same corner for 25 years. Radio at its best is just bringing a microphone to people who were never asked.
Thank you to the team at Johannesburg Metropolitan University for having me back to guest lecture on interview craft. Teaching the next generation keeps my own questions sharp.
A quiet reminder as the Off-Mic pre-launch ramps up: podcasting is not the death of radio, it is radio finding new rooms to sit in. I am not leaving the desk. I am building a second one.
Grateful for an early morning chat with Dr. Nomsa Khumalo about where Rhythm FM 94.7 is heading next. Twelve years in and I still love planning the sound of a station.
Five years ago I almost left radio. Today The Asha Show had its biggest morning yet. If you are in broadcasting and thinking of walking away, call me first. I have notes.
Looking for a South African community radio station doing brave local journalism on a shoestring. I want to feature you on Off-Mic. Drop a comment and tell me your story.

Speaking request season is open. If your media school, station, or conference wants a session on live radio, youth mentorship, or building a show that lasts, my inbox is open. Johannesburg based, happy to travel.

Off-air but never off the clock: spent the afternoon at Echo Room Studios listening back to the first Off-Mic test episode. There is a particular magic in hearing a story land exactly the way you hoped it would.
